---
title: "Phase C — Interactive simulator"
subtitle: "Live Monte Carlo on parameters of your choosing"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: false
---

```{=html}
<style>
  .phase-c-derived { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(180px, 1fr)); gap: 0.75rem; margin: 1rem 0; }
  .phase-c-derived .card { border: 1px solid #ddd; border-radius: 6px; padding: 0.6rem 0.75rem; background: #fff; }
  .phase-c-derived .card .label { font-size: 0.8rem; color: #555; }
  .phase-c-derived .card .value { font-family: ui-monospace, monospace; font-size: 1.1rem; }

  /* Section layout: body content stays at Quarto's native body-column
     width; the controls panel is pulled into the left gutter via a
     negative margin on the grid container. Avoiding a .column-* escape
     sidesteps Quarto's TOC-grid interactions (which were making the
     text column too wide and introducing an extra vertical gap).

     The left gutter's actual width can't be computed from 100vw alone:
     Quarto's page grid places the body column off-centre (TOC sits on
     the right), and the body column is narrower than a round viewport
     fraction. So --pc-left-gutter is measured at runtime from the body
     column's bounding rect (see <script> below). Default 0 keeps the
     sidebar collapsed until the measurement lands, so nothing bleeds
     off-screen on first paint. */
  .pc-section {
    --pc-gap: 1.5rem;
    --pc-left-gutter: 0px;
    --pc-sidebar-w: clamp(0px, calc(var(--pc-left-gutter) - 1rem), 420px);
    display: grid;
    grid-template-columns: var(--pc-sidebar-w) var(--pc-gap) minmax(0, 1fr);
    align-items: start;
    margin-left: calc(-1 * (var(--pc-sidebar-w) + var(--pc-gap)));
  }
  .pc-section > .pc-main { grid-column: 3; min-width: 0; }
  .pc-section > .pc-controls {
    grid-column: 1;
    position: sticky; top: 0.75rem;
    align-self: start;
    max-height: calc(100vh - 1.5rem);
    overflow-y: auto;
  }
  .pc-controls {
    padding: 0.75rem 1rem 1rem;
    border: 1px solid #ddd; border-radius: 8px; background: #fafafa;
    font-size: 0.85rem;
  }
  .pc-controls h3 { font-size: 0.9rem; margin: 0.9rem 0 0.4rem; color: #333; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.04em; border-bottom: 1px solid #e0e0e0; padding-bottom: 0.2rem; }
  .pc-controls p { font-size: 0.82rem; color: #555; margin: 0.25rem 0 0.6rem; }
  /* Align every slider row on a shared label column so the ranges and
     number boxes line up vertically. A fixed label width (wrapping when
     needed) is what makes the subsequent track + value columns start at
     the same x across rows. */
  .pc-controls form[class*="oi-"] {
    align-items: center;
  }
  .pc-controls form[class*="oi-"] > label {
    width: 11rem !important;
    min-width: 11rem !important;
    max-width: 11rem !important;
    white-space: normal !important;
    line-height: 1.2;
    flex-shrink: 0;
  }
  /* Give the numeric readout / select enough room that long values
     (e.g. 2,000,000 for inventory) don't get clipped with an ellipsis. */
  .pc-controls form[class*="oi-"] input[type="number"],
  .pc-controls form[class*="oi-"] select {
    min-width: 5.5rem;
  }
  .pc-controls form[class*="oi-"] input[type="range"] {
    min-width: 0;
    flex: 1 1 auto;
  }
  /* When JS has measured a gutter too small for a usable sidebar, or on
     narrow viewports, drop the grid, unstick the controls, reset margin,
     and release the fixed label column so labels can shrink with the
     now-stacked layout. .pc-narrow is toggled by the script below. */
  @media (max-width: 1100px) {
    .pc-section { display: block; margin-left: 0; }
    .pc-section > .pc-controls { position: static; max-height: none; }
    .pc-controls form[class*="oi-"] > label {
      width: auto !important;
      min-width: 90px !important;
      max-width: 160px !important;
    }
  }
  .pc-section.pc-narrow { display: block; margin-left: 0; }
  .pc-section.pc-narrow > .pc-controls { position: static; max-height: none; }
  .pc-section.pc-narrow .pc-controls form[class*="oi-"] > label {
    width: auto !important;
    min-width: 90px !important;
    max-width: 160px !important;
  }

  .pc-reset { margin-top: 1rem; padding-top: 0.75rem; border-top: 1px solid #e0e0e0; }
  .pc-reset button {
    width: 100%;
    padding: 0.4rem 0.7rem;
    border: 1px solid #888;
    background: #fff;
    color: #111;
    border-radius: 4px;
    cursor: pointer;
    font-size: 0.85rem;
    font-weight: 500;
  }
  .pc-reset button:hover { background: #eef; border-color: #555; }
</style>
<script>
  // Measure the actual left-gutter width of each .pc-section (the distance
  // from the viewport's left edge to the body column's left edge) and
  // expose it to CSS as --pc-left-gutter. We read the parent's bounding
  // rect rather than .pc-section's own, because the section has a negative
  // margin-left that would make its own rect.left meaningless. If the
  // gutter is below a usable threshold, mark the section .pc-narrow so
  // the stacked layout kicks in instead of a squashed sidebar.
  (function () {
    // Below this, the sidebar can't fit the fixed 11rem label column plus
    // a usable slider track + readout, so we stack instead.
    var MIN_GUTTER = 320;
    function update() {
      document.querySelectorAll('.pc-section').forEach(function (sec) {
        var parent = sec.parentElement;
        if (!parent) return;
        var left = parent.getBoundingClientRect().left;
        if (left < MIN_GUTTER) {
          sec.classList.add('pc-narrow');
          sec.style.setProperty('--pc-left-gutter', '0px');
        } else {
          sec.classList.remove('pc-narrow');
          sec.style.setProperty('--pc-left-gutter', left + 'px');
        }
      });
    }
    var schedule = function () { requestAnimationFrame(update); };
    if (document.readyState === 'loading') {
      document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', schedule);
    } else {
      schedule();
    }
    window.addEventListener('load', schedule);
    window.addEventListener('resize', schedule);
  })();
</script>
```

## What this page is about (for new readers)

The [Phase B notebook](phase-b.qmd) showed the same three business
designs — fee, fully back-to-back principal, partially pre-hedged
principal — under a **fixed** menu of parameters. It used a
pre-computed sweep over $(\alpha, \mu, \sigma)$ and a handful of
baked-in runs; the reader could pick a point on the grid, but could not
step outside it.

Phase C turns that notebook into a **live sandbox**. Every slider on
this page kicks off a fresh Monte Carlo in your browser (no server
round-trip, no JSON regeneration), so you can:

- Ask "what if the token starts twice as expensive?" and watch the
  whole distribution move, not just the pre-computed sample points.
- Seed the simulation with **real** inventory — say, 300,000 kVCM
  bought last quarter for \$21,000 — rather than the idealised
  "bought today at $S_0$" hedge the Phase B $\alpha$ parameter
  assumed.

For a deterministic complement — drawing a specific price trajectory
and reading its P&L straight off the curve — see the drawn-curve
scenario tool on the [Conclusions](conclusions.qmd) page.

The underlying price model is Geometric Brownian Motion
([Phase A](phase-a.qmd) §1) with an optional Merton jump-diffusion
overlay (leave the jump sliders at zero to recover pure GBM bit-for-bit)
and the same deterministic retirement flow. The GBM closed-form
anchors still apply to the means (the drift is compensated so
$\mathbb{E}[S_t]$ is unchanged by jumps); subsequent Phase C iterations
will swap in regime switching and Poisson demand behind the same UI.

::: {.callout-note-lite}
**Minimum background to read this page.** Familiarity with Monte Carlo
simulation and with basic distribution statistics (mean, standard
deviation, quantiles) is helpful. Every plot below has a "how to read
it" paragraph that states what a good-looking output looks like and
what a suspicious one would look like. If you want the underlying
formulas, the [Phase A note](phase-a.qmd) is the primary reference and
the [Phase B page](phase-b.qmd) carries the worked examples.
:::

## Starting Phase C

[Phase B](phase-b.qmd) cross-checked the closed-form §1–§5 quantities (see
the [Phase A note](phase-a.qmd)) against Monte Carlo and exposed a
**pre-baked** sweep over $(\alpha, \mu, \sigma)$ through the Observable
notebook. Phase C extends the modelling menu — jump diffusion, regime
switching, Poisson retirements, historical calibration, dynamic hedging —
and the first piece of scaffolding it needs is **a way to run the
simulator live on parameters of our choosing**, not just a pre-computed grid.

This page is that scaffolding. Every slider below triggers a fresh Monte
Carlo in the browser (no server round-trip, no artifact regeneration). The
baseline price process is the same GBM used in [Phase B](phase-b.qmd),
now with an optional Merton jump-diffusion overlay exposed through three
jump sliders (leave them at zero to recover pure GBM); subsequent Phase
C iterations will swap in the remaining richer dynamics behind the same
UI.

### What the page actually does

This page has two blocks, in reading order:

1. **A parameter panel** (the sticky sidebar on the left, or stacked
   above the plots on a narrow screen). Every input the simulator takes
   is a slider or a drop-down. Defaults are chosen to match a
   mid-of-the-road Phase B scenario so the initial view is
   directly comparable to the pre-baked figures there.
2. **A live Monte Carlo block.** Given the sliders, the browser runs
   $n_{\mathrm{paths}}$ full GBM trajectories, computes the three
   books' terminal P&L on each, and summarises the resulting
   distributions (summary table + histograms + a handful of sample
   price paths). Changing any slider re-runs the whole block in real
   time.

For a deterministic-scenario complement — fix **one** specific
trajectory by sketching it and read the three books' P&L on that exact
curve — see the [Conclusions](conclusions.qmd) page.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
## What changes vs. [Phase B](phase-b.qmd)

The pre-purchase hedge is now specified by the **raw inventory state** —
a number of kVCM tokens and their USD cost basis — instead of the ratio
$\alpha$. This removes the Phase B assumption that the tokens were
bought today at $S_0$: you can seed the simulation with an inventory
that was acquired earlier at a different price. The Phase B $(\alpha =
k / (N \cdot P),\; \text{basis} = \alpha \cdot N \cdot P \cdot S_0)$
parametrisation is a special case of the new one.

The practical payoff of this change: the operator can ask questions
Phase B couldn't, such as *"how much does it matter that my existing
inventory was acquired at \$0.07 per token rather than today's
\$0.10?"* (Answer: it shifts the principal book's P&L by a constant —
the basis — but does not change the **shape** of its distribution,
because the basis is a sunk cost at $t = 0$.) The shape/location split
becomes visible in the summary table below: running the same $(\mu,
\sigma, T, \lambda, Q, f)$ with different inventory basis leaves the
SD, VaR, and CVaR of the custom-principal book unchanged and shifts
only the mean.
:::

### Mini-glossary

Three concepts recur throughout this page. If you've read the
[Phase B mini-glossary](phase-b.qmd), the first two are unchanged; the
third is the new Phase C concept.

- **Geometric Brownian Motion (GBM).** A standard model for prices
  that move by random **percentage** jumps, not random dollar jumps.
  Parameterised by a drift $\mu$ (expected growth rate per year) and a
  volatility $\sigma$ (size of the random jitter per year). Token
  prices, and the dollar-per-tonne figure $\pi_t = P \cdot S_t$, both
  follow this process in the baseline model.
- **Monte Carlo simulation.** Rolling the dice thousands of times.
  Each "roll" here is a full random trajectory of the token price
  from $t = 0$ to $t = T$, and on each roll we compute the terminal
  P&L of every book. The distribution of those outcomes is what every
  histogram and summary statistic on this page is built from.
- **Raw inventory state $(k, C_{\mathrm{basis}})$.** Two numbers that
  fully describe whatever tokens the operator already holds on day
  zero: $k$ is how many kVCM tokens they have, $C_{\mathrm{basis}}$
  is how many dollars they paid for them (a sunk cost). In Phase B
  the hedge was specified by a single ratio $\alpha$, which
  implicitly fixed both: $k = \alpha \cdot N \cdot P$ and
  $C_{\mathrm{basis}} = k \cdot S_0$. The Phase C form lifts the
  "bought today at $S_0$" assumption so inventory acquired earlier
  (at a different price, or with a different amount) can be studied
  directly.

### Notation cheat-sheet

A brief reminder of the symbols used on this page (full derivations in
the [Phase A note](phase-a.qmd) §1–§5; Phase B uses the same symbols
with $\alpha$ taking the role of the inventory state). Read the table
once top-to-bottom the first time you open this page; after that, the
formulas should parse on sight.

| Symbol | Meaning | Units |
| --- | --- | --- |
| $S_t$ | kVCM / USD spot price at time $t$ | USD / kVCM |
| $S_0$ | starting spot price | USD / kVCM |
| $\pi_0$ | starting USD protocol price per tonne of CO₂ | USD / tonne |
| $P := \pi_0 / S_0$ | protocol constant: kVCM needed per tonne | kVCM / tonne |
| $\mu$ | GBM drift (annualised) | 1 / year |
| $\sigma^2$ | GBM variance (annualised); $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | 1 / year |
| $\lambda_J$ | Merton jump intensity (expected jumps per year) | 1 / year |
| $\mu_J$ | mean of the log-jump $\log J \sim N(\mu_J, \sigma_J^2)$ | dimensionless |
| $\sigma_J$ | SD of the log-jump | dimensionless |
| $\lambda_{\mathrm{day}}$ | retirement flow specified to the UI | tonnes / day |
| $\lambda = 365 \cdot \lambda_{\mathrm{day}}$ | same flow, internal year unit | tonnes / year |
| $T$ | horizon (days in UI, years internally) | days / years |
| $N := \lambda \cdot T$ | total tonnes retired over $[0, T]$ | tonnes |
| $k$ | pre-purchased inventory | kVCM |
| $C_{\mathrm{basis}}$ | sunk cost of that inventory | USD |
| $N \cdot P$ | tokens needed to meet total demand | kVCM |
| $\tau := \min(1,\; k / (N \cdot P))$ | fraction of horizon the inventory covers | dimensionless |
| $k_{\mathrm{left}} := \max(0,\; k - N \cdot P)$ | leftover tokens at horizon (over-hedge) | kVCM |
| $I_T := \int_0^T S_t\,dt$ | full-horizon time-integral of the price | (USD/kVCM)·year |
| $J_\tau := \int_{\tau T}^{T} S_t\,dt$ | tail integral over the uncovered-by-inventory window | (USD/kVCM)·year |
| $R_{\mathrm{fee}} = f \cdot P \cdot \lambda \cdot I_T$ | fee-based revenue | USD |
| $\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}} = Q \cdot N - P \cdot \lambda \cdot I_T$ | back-to-back principal P&L | USD |
| $\Pi_{\mathrm{cust}} = Q \cdot N - C_{\mathrm{basis}} - P \cdot \lambda \cdot J_\tau + k_{\mathrm{left}} \cdot S_T$ | custom-inventory principal P&L | USD |
| $Q^* = (1+f) \cdot P \cdot S_0 \cdot (e^{\mu T}-1)/(\mu T)$ | break-even quote equating $\mathbb{E}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}] = \mathbb{E}[R_{\mathrm{fee}}]$ | USD / tonne |

Quick reading guide for the Phase-C-specific symbols:

- **$\tau$ ("coverage fraction")** is how far into the horizon the
  pre-purchased inventory lasts if it is burned at the deterministic
  rate $\lambda$. $\tau = 1$ means fully covered (§3a matched book);
  $\tau = 0$ means no pre-buy (§3b back-to-back); $\tau \in (0, 1)$
  means partial cover, and the operator buys the remainder at spot
  between $\tau T$ and $T$.
- **$J_\tau$** is the time-integral of the price **only over the
  uncovered tail** $[\tau T,\, T]$. It is the custom book's analogue
  of Phase B's $I_T$: when $\tau = 0$, $J_\tau = I_T$ (back-to-back);
  when $\tau = 1$, $J_\tau = 0$ (fully matched).
- **$k_{\mathrm{left}}$** is non-zero only when the operator
  **over-hedged** — bought more tokens than the year's demand. Those
  leftover tokens are carried to the horizon and marked-to-market at
  $S_T$. This is why the custom book's P&L acquires a random
  $k_{\mathrm{left}} \cdot S_T$ term in the over-hedge regime: a
  different flavour of exposure from the undersupply one, but still
  random.

:::: {.pc-section}

::: {.pc-controls}
### Price process

Sets the four numbers that define the GBM model of the token price.
$\pi_0$ is the carbon price expressed in **USD per tonne** and $P$ is
the same price expressed in **kVCM per tonne** (the protocol constant
— how many tokens are burned to retire one tonne); together they imply
today's spot $S_0 = \pi_0 / P$. $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ are the
annualised expected return and variance of the log-price.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pi0 = Inputs.range([0, 500], { step: 1, value: 125, label: "π₀ (USD / tonne)" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof P = Inputs.range([100, 25000], { step: 10, value: 2160, label: "P (kVCM / tonne)" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof mu = Inputs.range([-1, 1], { step: 0.005, value: -0.1, label: "drift μ (annualised)" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof sigma2 = Inputs.range([0, 3], { step: 0.005, value: 0.06, label: "variance σ² (annualised)" })
```

### Jumps (Merton)

Optional discrete shocks layered on top of the GBM. $\lambda_J$ is the
expected number of jumps per year; when a jump arrives the log-price
receives an independent $N(\mu_J, \sigma_J^2)$ shock. The drift is
**compensated** ($\mu$ absorbs $-\lambda_J \kappa$ with
$\kappa = e^{\mu_J + \sigma_J^2/2} - 1$) so $\mathbb{E}[S_t] = S_0 e^{\mu t}$
stays put regardless of the jump settings — jumps widen the
distribution without relocating it. Leaving $\lambda_J = 0$ collapses
to pure GBM.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof lambdaJ = Inputs.range([0, 50], { step: 0.1, value: 20, label: "jump intensity λ_J (/yr)" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof muJ = Inputs.range([-0.5, 0.5], { step: 0.01, value: -0.05, label: "mean log-jump μ_J" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof sigmaJ = Inputs.range([0, 1], { step: 0.01, value: 0.35, label: "log-jump SD σ_J" })
```

### Demand & inventory

Retirement flow $\lambda$ (tonnes per calendar day) plus the raw
starting inventory state $(k,\, C_{\mathrm{basis}})$. Coverage of the
horizon by the inventory is computed automatically and shown in the
live readout below.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof lambdaDay = Inputs.range([0, 50], { step: 0.1, value: 1, label: "retirements (tonnes / day)" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof kPre = Inputs.range([0, 2000000], { step: 1000, value: 300000, label: "initial inventory (kVCM)" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof cBasis = Inputs.range([0, 1000000], { step: 100, value: 21000, label: "inventory basis (USD, total)" })
```

### Contract

Horizon (in calendar days for UI convenience; converted internally to
years) and the two business levers: the fixed USD quote per tonne $Q$
of the principal book, and the proportional fee $f$ of the fee book.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof Tdays = Inputs.range([30, 1095], { step: 5, value: 365, label: "horizon T (days)" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof Q = Inputs.range([0, 1000], { step: 0.5, value: 180, label: "fixed quote Q (USD / tonne)" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof fee = Inputs.range([0, 1], { step: 0.01, value: 0.4, label: "fee rate f" })
```

### Monte Carlo

Knobs for the simulator itself — how many random trajectories, how
many time-steps per trajectory, and the PRNG seed. These are
**statistical** controls (more paths = tighter estimates), not
business controls.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof nPaths = Inputs.select([2000, 5000, 10000, 20000, 40000], { value: 5000, label: "paths" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof nSteps = Inputs.select([50, 100, 200, 365], { value: 100, label: "steps / path" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof seed = Inputs.range([1, 9999], { step: 1, value: 42, label: "PRNG seed" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
mcResetButton = {
  const defaults = {
    pi0: 125, P: 2160, mu: -0.1, sigma2: 0.06,
    lambdaJ: 20, muJ: -0.05, sigmaJ: 0.35,
    lambdaDay: 1, kPre: 300000, cBasis: 21000,
    Tdays: 365, Q: 180, fee: 0.4,
    nPaths: 5000, nSteps: 100, seed: 42,
  };
  const views = {
    pi0: viewof pi0, P: viewof P, mu: viewof mu, sigma2: viewof sigma2,
    lambdaJ: viewof lambdaJ, muJ: viewof muJ, sigmaJ: viewof sigmaJ,
    lambdaDay: viewof lambdaDay, kPre: viewof kPre, cBasis: viewof cBasis,
    Tdays: viewof Tdays, Q: viewof Q, fee: viewof fee,
    nPaths: viewof nPaths, nSteps: viewof nSteps, seed: viewof seed,
  };
  const wrap = document.createElement("div");
  wrap.className = "pc-reset";
  const btn = document.createElement("button");
  btn.type = "button";
  btn.textContent = "Reset to defaults";
  btn.addEventListener("click", () => {
    for (const k of Object.keys(defaults)) {
      const el = views[k];
      el.value = defaults[k];
      el.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent("input", { bubbles: true }));
    }
  });
  wrap.appendChild(btn);
  return wrap;
}
```

:::

::: {.pc-main}

Drift $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$ are annualised. The retirement rate
$\lambda$ is specified per calendar day; internally we use a year as the
time unit, so $\lambda_{\mathrm{yr}} = 365 \cdot \lambda_{\mathrm{day}}$.
Defaults are set so that $Q$ tracks the Phase B fee-equivalent quote at
$t = 0$; override to study off-market pricing.

### What each slider controls, in plain English

The sidebar groups inputs by their role in the model. A short
walk-through of each group, keyed to what you will see change in the
plots below when you move the slider.

**Price process** (four knobs).

- **$\pi_0$ (USD per tonne).** Today's dollar price of retiring one
  tonne. Moving this rescales the whole simulation but does not
  change any P&L shape — it re-prices both the fee book's revenue
  and the principal book's quote equally.
- **$P$ (kVCM per tonne).** The **protocol constant** — the number
  of tokens the protocol needs per tonne of CO₂ retired. Together
  with $\pi_0$ it implies today's token spot $S_0 = \pi_0 / P$
  (printed in the live readout below). $P$ is a constant of the
  protocol and does not move during the simulation; moving the
  slider changes how much of the per-tonne USD price is denominated
  in tokens versus dollars.
- **$\mu$ (annualised drift).** The expected growth rate of the
  token price under GBM. Positive $\mu$ pushes the principal book
  into the red on average (it is structurally short the token);
  negative $\mu$ benefits it.
- **$\sigma^2$ (annualised variance).** The size of the random
  jitter in log-returns. Moving this leaves the mean of every book
  unchanged but inflates the standard deviation and fattens the
  tails. Use the "VaR / CVaR" columns in the summary table below to
  watch the effect.

**Jumps (Merton)** (three knobs — this is the first Phase-C roadmap
extension to land; the baseline GBM is recovered by leaving
$\lambda_J = 0$).

- **$\lambda_J$ (jumps / year).** How frequently a discrete shock
  hits. At $\lambda_J = 0$ the process is pure GBM; at $\lambda_J =
  4$ the price takes on average four Poisson-arrival shocks over a
  one-year horizon. Raising $\lambda_J$ widens every book's
  distribution.
- **$\mu_J$ (mean log-jump).** The centre of the log-jump
  distribution. $\mu_J = 0$ gives symmetric shocks; $\mu_J < 0$
  tilts the jumps toward crashes (each arrival multiplies the price
  by roughly $e^{\mu_J}$), which fattens the **left** tail of the
  principal books and the **right** tail of the fee book. Because
  the drift is compensated, every book's mean stays put — only the
  shape changes.
- **$\sigma_J$ (log-jump SD).** How dispersed individual jumps are
  around $\mu_J$. Raising $\sigma_J$ deepens the tails on both
  sides.

**Demand & inventory** (three knobs — this is the Phase-C-specific
block).

- **$\lambda_{\mathrm{day}}$ (tonnes per day).** The constant
  retirement flow. Doubling $\lambda$ doubles $N = \lambda T$ and
  therefore roughly doubles every book's mean and standard
  deviation — but does not change their shape, nor the Sharpe-like
  ratio.
- **$k$ (kVCM tokens held).** How many tokens are already on the
  balance sheet at $t = 0$. This is the **inventory state** Phase
  B reduced to a single ratio $\alpha$. Moving $k$ changes the
  coverage fraction $\tau$, which in turn changes the length of the
  uncovered tail integral $J_\tau$ and hence the width of the
  custom book's distribution.
- **$C_{\mathrm{basis}}$ (USD, total).** What that inventory cost to
  acquire. Because the basis is a sunk cost at $t = 0$, moving this
  slider shifts the custom book's distribution **along the x-axis
  by a constant** (by $-\Delta C_{\mathrm{basis}}$) and leaves its
  shape unchanged.

**Contract** (three knobs).

- **$T$ (days).** Horizon length. The internal time unit is years,
  so $T_{\mathrm{yr}} = T_{\mathrm{days}} / 365$. Longer horizons
  let drift compound (the $Q^*$ ratio below diverges from 1) and
  increase the total tonnes $N = \lambda T$.
- **$Q$ (USD per tonne).** The fixed price the principal book
  quotes. Moving $Q$ translates the principal book's P&L by
  $\Delta Q \cdot N$ — a pure parallel shift. The break-even value
  at which $\mathbb{E}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}] = \mathbb{E}[R_{\mathrm{fee}}]$
  is $Q^*$ (printed in the derived-quantities card below).
- **$f$ (fee rate).** The fee book's markup. Moving $f$ rescales
  the fee book's distribution linearly (doubling $f$ doubles both
  mean and SD, leaving Sharpe invariant). It has **no effect** on
  the principal books — they don't use $f$ at all.

**Monte Carlo** (three knobs).

- **paths.** Number of random GBM trajectories. Statistical error
  on every estimate scales like $1/\sqrt{n_{\mathrm{paths}}}$, so
  quadrupling paths halves the confidence-interval half-width. The
  browser's typical speed budget here is ~10k–40k paths in well
  under a second; we default to 5,000 for slider responsiveness.
- **steps / path.** Number of time-steps used to discretise each
  path. The simulator accumulates $I_T$ and $J_\tau$ by the
  trapezoid rule, so discretisation error is $O(1/n_{\mathrm{steps}}^2)$
  — 100 steps already under-counts bias by orders of magnitude
  relative to Monte Carlo noise.
- **seed.** Deterministic seed for the Mulberry32 PRNG. Same
  `(seed, inputs)` pair = same numerical result on every reload,
  which is the only thing that makes "this VaR number" reproducible
  across reruns.

### Coverage: the one derived quantity that drives the custom book

Of all the derived quantities below, **coverage** $\tau$ is the one
worth pausing on. The operator has $k$ tokens on hand; the year will
consume $N \cdot P$ tokens at the steady rate $\lambda$. Coverage is
$\tau = \min(1,\, k / (N \cdot P))$ — the fraction of the horizon for
which the inventory lasts. Three regimes, all exposed by the same
sliders:

1. **Under-hedged ($\tau < 1$).** Inventory runs out at $t = \tau
   T$; from then on, retirements are sourced back-to-back at spot.
   The custom book's randomness lives in the tail integral
   $J_\tau = \int_{\tau T}^{T} S_t\,dt$. As $\tau \to 1$, this tail
   shrinks and the custom book tends toward the matched book.
2. **Exactly matched ($\tau = 1$, $k = N \cdot P$).** The tail
   integral collapses to zero, so the P&L is $Q \cdot N -
   C_{\mathrm{basis}}$, **deterministic**. This reproduces
   Phase B's §3a point.
3. **Over-hedged ($\tau = 1$, $k > N \cdot P$).** Enough tokens to
   cover the year, with leftover $k_{\mathrm{left}} = k - N \cdot P$
   tokens carried to the horizon. Those leftovers are marked-to-
   market at $S_T$, so the P&L reacquires randomness — but through
   a **point estimate** of the price ($S_T$), not an integral. This
   is structurally different from under-hedged risk and the
   distribution shape reflects that: terminal-price risk looks
   log-normal rather than integrated-log-normal.

The next section prints $\tau$ live and flags when it hits 100%.

## Derived quantities

Everything in the scorecard below is computed from the sliders by a
single closed-form expression (no Monte Carlo needed). The point of
displaying them is to turn the sliders' raw numbers into quantities
that appear in the equations — so you can sanity-check the setup
before looking at the MC output.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
derived = {
  const sigma = Math.sqrt(sigma2);
  const S0 = P > 0 ? pi0 / P : 0;
  const T = Tdays / 365;
  const lambda = lambdaDay * 365;
  const N = lambda * T;
  const tokensNeeded = N * P;
  const coverageDays = (lambda > 0 && P > 0) ? kPre / (lambda * P) * 365 : Infinity;
  const coverageFrac = tokensNeeded > 0 ? Math.min(1, kPre / tokensNeeded) : 0;
  const impliedCost = kPre > 0 ? cBasis / kPre : 0;
  const QStarRatio = expm1OverX(mu * T);
  const QStar = (1 + fee) * P * S0 * QStarRatio;
  return { sigma, S0, T, lambda, N, tokensNeeded, coverageDays, coverageFrac, impliedCost, QStar };
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
html`<div class="phase-c-derived">
  <div class="card"><div class="label">σ (volatility)</div><div class="value">${derived.sigma.toFixed(4)}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">S₀ = π₀ / P (USD / kVCM)</div><div class="value">${"$" + derived.S0.toFixed(4)}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">T (years)</div><div class="value">${derived.T.toFixed(3)}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">N = λ·T (tonnes over horizon)</div><div class="value">${derived.N.toLocaleString(undefined, {maximumFractionDigits: 0})}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">tokens needed N·P</div><div class="value">${derived.tokensNeeded.toLocaleString(undefined, {maximumFractionDigits: 0})}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">coverage from inventory</div><div class="value">${(derived.coverageFrac * 100).toFixed(1)}%</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">inventory runs out at (days)</div><div class="value">${isFinite(derived.coverageDays) ? Math.min(derived.coverageDays, Tdays).toFixed(1) : "∞"}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">implied basis per token</div><div class="value">${kPre > 0 ? "$" + derived.impliedCost.toFixed(3) : "—"}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">break-even Q* (USD / tonne)</div><div class="value">${"$" + derived.QStar.toFixed(3)}</div></div>
</div>`
```

### How to read the cards above

- **$\sigma$.** Simply $\sqrt{\sigma^2}$. Displayed because the sim
  internally uses $\sigma$ (the SD), not $\sigma^2$ (the variance), and
  it is the number that appears in every moment formula.
- **$S_0 = \pi_0 / P$.** Today's token spot price in USD / kVCM,
  derived from the carbon price $\pi_0$ (USD / tonne) and the protocol
  constant $P$ (kVCM / tonne). Both are first-class sliders, so the
  user can keep the dollar price per tonne fixed and independently
  vary where it lives in token space.
- **$T$ (years).** The horizon in the internal time unit. Multiply any
  per-year quantity by this to translate to the horizon total.
- **$N = \lambda T$.** Total tonnes the business will retire over the
  horizon. This is the multiplier that connects per-tonne quantities
  ($Q$, $\pi_0$) to total P&L.
- **Tokens needed $N \cdot P$.** The *physical* token demand over the
  horizon. Compare this number against the inventory $k$ to read off
  coverage mentally.
- **Coverage.** $\tau = \min(1,\, k / (N \cdot P))$, expressed as a
  percentage. At 100% the custom book is §3a-deterministic; below 100%
  the tail of the horizon is back-to-back at spot.
- **Inventory runs out at.** When coverage is below 100%, this is
  $\tau \cdot T$ expressed in days — the calendar day on which the
  last pre-purchased token is burned. After that day the operator is
  paying spot for the remainder.
- **Implied basis per token.** $C_{\mathrm{basis}} / k$ — the average
  USD price per token that was paid for the existing inventory. If
  this number sits above the current $S_0$, the operator is carrying
  an **unrealised loss** on the inventory; below $S_0$, an unrealised
  gain.
- **Break-even $Q^*$.** The quote at which the fee book and the
  back-to-back principal book have equal **expected** P&L (Phase A
  §4; the formula is $(1+f) \cdot P \cdot S_0 \cdot (e^{\mu T} -
  1)/(\mu T)$). Handy as a reference point when choosing $Q$: setting
  $Q = Q^*$ equalises means; the sections below make clear how
  *distributions* differ despite the matched means.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
## Interpreting "coverage"

If the pre-purchased inventory covers 100% of the horizon's token
demand, the book is fully matched (§3a-style). Below 100%, the operator
runs out of inventory part-way through the horizon and has to buy the
remainder back-to-back at spot — that is where the left-tail risk
appears. Above 100% (over-hedged) the excess tokens are marked-to-market
at $S_T$ at the horizon.

**Why the two regimes (under- and over-hedge) produce different-shaped
loss distributions.** Under-hedged, the uncovered tail is a *path
integral* $J_\tau = \int_{\tau T}^{T} S_t\,dt$ — a smoothed average of
many price points. Over-hedged, the residual exposure is
$k_{\mathrm{left}} \cdot S_T$ — the price at a **single point** in
time. Integrals of log-normals are less heavy-tailed than a raw
log-normal (an averaging effect), so the shape of the under-hedge
loss tail is milder than the over-hedge gain/loss tail at the same
dollar exposure. That asymmetry shows up in the VaR / CVaR columns of
the summary table below when you push $k$ past $N \cdot P$.
:::

## Core simulator

All cells below are pure JavaScript running in the browser — no server, no
pre-computed JSON. The simulator is a direct port of `src/rng.ts`,
`src/gbm.ts`, `src/moments.ts`, and `src/risk.ts`. Keeping it inline lets
readers audit every calculation in one place.

### What the simulator computes, step-by-step

Every slider change triggers the following sequence:

1. **Seed the PRNG.** A Mulberry32 generator is initialised from the
   current `seed` slider. The generator emits uniform $U(0,1)$ draws;
   Box–Muller converts pairs of uniforms into standard normals.
2. **Simulate $n_{\mathrm{paths}}$ GBM (or jump-GBM) trajectories.**
   Each trajectory steps the price forward $n_{\mathrm{steps}}$
   times using the log-GBM increment $S_{t + dt} = S_t \cdot
   \exp\!\big((\mu - \tfrac12 \sigma^2 - \lambda_J \kappa)\,dt +
   \sigma\sqrt{dt}\,Z + \sum_{j=1}^{N_{t+dt} - N_t} Y_j\big)$, where
   $N$ is a Poisson process with intensity $\lambda_J$, each
   $Y_j \sim N(\mu_J, \sigma_J^2)$, and
   $\kappa = e^{\mu_J + \sigma_J^2/2} - 1$ is the Merton
   compensation term. With $\lambda_J = 0$ the jump sum is empty and
   this reduces to the same recipe as `src/gbm.ts`.
3. **Accumulate the integrals.** On each trajectory we compute
   (a) the full-horizon integral $I_T = \int_0^T S_t\,dt$ by the
   trapezoid rule, and (b) the tail integral $J_\tau = \int_{\tau
   T}^T S_t\,dt$ over the window where inventory has already run
   out. (b) is only nonzero when coverage is below 100%.
4. **Plug the integrals into each book's formula.** Fee: $f P
   \lambda I_T$. Back-to-back: $Q N - P \lambda I_T$. Custom: $Q N
   - C_{\mathrm{basis}} - P \lambda J_\tau + k_{\mathrm{left}} S_T$.
5. **Summarise.** Mean, standard deviation, 95/99 VaR and CVaR, loss
   probability, and a Sharpe-like ratio are computed from the
   sample array. These are the numbers populating the table below.
6. **Keep a handful of paths for plotting.** Only the first ~20
   trajectories are stored in full; the rest contribute their
   scalar P&L and are discarded to keep memory flat.

The implementation below is deliberately inline and un-minified. It is
safe to click through every cell: nothing writes to disk, nothing calls
out to the network, and refreshing the page is a clean reset.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
function makeRng(seedVal) {
  let state = seedVal >>> 0;
  let cached = null;
  const uniform = () => {
    state = (state + 0x6d2b79f5) >>> 0;
    let t = state;
    t = Math.imul(t ^ (t >>> 15), t | 1);
    t ^= t + Math.imul(t ^ (t >>> 7), t | 61);
    const u = ((t ^ (t >>> 14)) >>> 0) / 4294967296;
    return u === 0 ? 1 / 4294967296 : u;
  };
  const normal = () => {
    if (cached !== null) {
      const z = cached;
      cached = null;
      return z;
    }
    const u1 = uniform();
    const u2 = uniform();
    const r = Math.sqrt(-2 * Math.log(u1));
    const th = 2 * Math.PI * u2;
    cached = r * Math.sin(th);
    return r * Math.cos(th);
  };
  // Knuth's product-of-uniforms Poisson sampler. Always called here with
  // lambda = λ_J · dt ≪ 1, so the loop resolves in one or two iterations.
  const poisson = (lambda) => {
    if (!(lambda > 0)) return 0;
    const L = Math.exp(-lambda);
    let k = 0;
    let p = 1;
    for (;;) {
      k++;
      p *= uniform();
      if (p <= L) return k - 1;
    }
  };
  return { uniform, normal, poisson };
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
function expm1OverX(x) {
  if (x === 0) return 1;
  if (Math.abs(x) < 1e-8) return 1 + x / 2 + (x * x) / 6;
  return Math.expm1(x) / x;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
function quantileSorted(sorted, q) {
  const n = sorted.length;
  if (n === 0) return NaN;
  if (n === 1) return sorted[0];
  const pos = q * (n - 1);
  const lo = Math.floor(pos);
  const hi = Math.ceil(pos);
  if (lo === hi) return sorted[lo];
  const frac = pos - lo;
  return sorted[lo] * (1 - frac) + sorted[hi] * frac;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
function tailMean(sortedAscending, q) {
  const n = sortedAscending.length;
  const k = Math.max(1, Math.ceil((1 - q) * n));
  let s = 0;
  for (let i = 0; i < k; i++) s += sortedAscending[i];
  return s / k;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
// Pick calendar-ish tick positions (in days) so the x-axis steps in
// whole weeks / months / years depending on the horizon, rather than
// rescaling to a fixed number of evenly-spaced ticks. The same helper
// drives the drawable canvas and the two Plot charts below so all three
// agree on where the gridlines land.
xTicksForHorizon = function (tdays) {
  let step;
  if (tdays <= 60) step = 7;
  else if (tdays <= 730) step = 30;
  else step = 365;
  const ticks = [];
  for (let d = 0; d <= tdays; d += step) ticks.push(d);
  return ticks;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
// Same step logic as xTicksForHorizon but anchored on the right edge
// (day = tdays) rather than zero. Used by the custom-curve chart whose
// x-axis maps day = tdays to "today" and day = 0 to "T days ago", so
// the rightmost tick always lands on the build-time anchor regardless
// of how T divides the step.
xTicksAnchoredRight = function (tdays) {
  let step;
  if (tdays <= 60) step = 7;
  else if (tdays <= 730) step = 30;
  else step = 365;
  const ticks = [];
  for (let d = tdays; d >= 0; d -= step) ticks.push(d);
  return ticks.reverse();
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
// Render a date at a resolution appropriate for the horizon it sits on:
// day+month for weekly ticks, month+year for monthly ticks, year only
// for yearly ticks.
formatTickDate = function (date, tdays) {
  if (tdays <= 60) {
    return date.toLocaleString("en-US", { month: "short", day: "numeric" });
  }
  if (tdays <= 730) {
    return date.toLocaleString("en-US", { month: "short", year: "2-digit" });
  }
  return date.toLocaleString("en-US", { year: "numeric" });
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
function simulate(inputs) {
  const {
    S0, mu, sigma, P, lambda, T, Q, fee, kPre, cBasis,
    lambdaJ, muJ, sigmaJ,
    nPaths, nSteps, seed,
  } = inputs;
  const rng = makeRng(seed);
  const dt = T / nSteps;
  // Compensated Merton drift: subtracting λ_J·κ keeps E[S_t] = S_0·e^{μt}
  // regardless of jump settings, so turning jumps on widens the
  // distributions without shifting their means.
  const kappa = lambdaJ > 0
    ? Math.exp(muJ + 0.5 * sigmaJ * sigmaJ) - 1
    : 0;
  const drift = (mu - 0.5 * sigma * sigma - lambdaJ * kappa) * dt;
  const diffusion = sigma * Math.sqrt(dt);
  const lamDt = lambdaJ * dt;
  const N = lambda * T;
  // Fraction of horizon over which inventory lasts, clamped to [0, 1].
  // Retirements are a constant flow λ, so `kPre` tokens cover
  // τ = kPre / (λ P) years of demand.
  const tauFrac = (lambda > 0 && P > 0)
    ? Math.min(1, kPre / (lambda * P * T))
    : 1;
  const tokensUsedInternal = Math.min(kPre, lambda * T * P);
  const tokensLeftover = Math.max(0, kPre - lambda * T * P);
  // When inventory covers the whole horizon, the tail integral range [τT, T]
  // has zero length; park tailStartStep beyond the loop so no node ever
  // contributes. (Math.ceil(1 · nSteps) = nSteps would otherwise add
  // 0.5·S_T·dt from the single collapsed endpoint.)
  // Snap tauFrac onto the integration grid and treat "only the endpoint
  // falls in [τT, T]" as an empty range — otherwise the trapezoid rule
  // would contribute 0.5·S_T·dt for a zero-length interval.
  const tailStartRaw = Math.ceil(tauFrac * nSteps);
  const tailStartStep = tailStartRaw >= nSteps ? nSteps + 1 : tailStartRaw;

  const feeSamples = new Float64Array(nPaths);
  const principalSamples = new Float64Array(nPaths);
  const b2bSamples = new Float64Array(nPaths);
  const ITSamples = new Float64Array(nPaths);
  const terminalS = new Float64Array(nPaths);
  const keepPaths = Math.min(20, nPaths);
  const sampledPaths = [];

  for (let i = 0; i < nPaths; i++) {
    // Trapezoid accumulators: full-horizon I_T and uncovered-tail integral
    // ∫_{τ T}^{T} S_t dt. We stitch the tail integral out of the same
    // trajectory so it uses the same realisations as I_T.
    let S = S0;
    let IT = 0;
    let tailInt = 0;
    const path = (i < keepPaths) ? new Float64Array(nSteps + 1) : null;
    if (path) path[0] = S;

    // Trapezoid rule: weight endpoints by 1/2.
    IT += 0.5 * S;
    if (tailStartStep === 0) tailInt += 0.5 * S;

    for (let k = 1; k <= nSteps; k++) {
      const z = rng.normal();
      let jumpSum = 0;
      if (lambdaJ > 0) {
        const nJ = rng.poisson(lamDt);
        for (let j = 0; j < nJ; j++) {
          jumpSum += muJ + sigmaJ * rng.normal();
        }
      }
      S = S * Math.exp(drift + diffusion * z + jumpSum);
      if (path) path[k] = S;
      const w = (k === nSteps) ? 0.5 : 1;
      IT += w * S;
      if (k >= tailStartStep) {
        const wt = (k === tailStartStep || k === nSteps) ? 0.5 : 1;
        tailInt += wt * S;
      }
    }
    IT *= dt;
    tailInt *= dt;
    terminalS[i] = S;
    ITSamples[i] = IT;

    // Fee book: R_fee = f · P · λ · I_T.
    feeSamples[i] = fee * P * lambda * IT;

    // Back-to-back principal book (no pre-purchase): Π = Q·N − P·λ·I_T.
    b2bSamples[i] = Q * N - P * lambda * IT;

    // Custom principal with initial inventory:
    //   Π = Q·N − C_basis − P·λ·∫_{τT}^{T} S_t dt + leftover · S_T.
    // The cost basis is a sunk cost at t=0; uncovered retirements after
    // inventory exhaustion are bought at spot; excess tokens mark-to-market
    // at horizon.
    principalSamples[i] =
      Q * N - cBasis - P * lambda * tailInt + tokensLeftover * S;

    if (path) sampledPaths.push(path);
  }

  return {
    feeSamples,
    principalSamples,
    b2bSamples,
    ITSamples,
    terminalS,
    sampledPaths,
    tauFrac,
    tokensUsedInternal,
    tokensLeftover,
    N,
  };
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
function summarise(samples) {
  const n = samples.length;
  let sum = 0;
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) sum += samples[i];
  const mean = sum / n;
  let sse = 0;
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const d = samples[i] - mean;
    sse += d * d;
  }
  const variance = n > 1 ? sse / (n - 1) : 0;
  const sd = Math.sqrt(variance);
  const stderr = sd / Math.sqrt(n);
  const sorted = Float64Array.from(samples); sorted.sort();
  const var95 = -quantileSorted(sorted, 0.05);
  const var99 = -quantileSorted(sorted, 0.01);
  const cvar95 = -tailMean(sorted, 0.95);
  const cvar99 = -tailMean(sorted, 0.99);
  let losses = 0;
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) if (samples[i] < 0) losses++;
  const probLoss = losses / n;
  const sharpe = sd > 0 ? mean / sd : null;
  return { mean, sd, stderr, ci95: 1.96 * stderr, var95, var99, cvar95, cvar99, probLoss, sharpe };
}
```

## Monte Carlo run

This section is the live output of the simulator. The run card just
below shows how many paths were generated and how long the run took;
the three subsections that follow present the results three ways — a
summary table (numbers), histograms (distributions), and a handful of
sample price paths (individual trajectories).

Keep an eye on **wall time** as you change sliders: the simulator is
fast enough that you can generally keep paths at 5,000 or 10,000 and
still get sub-second updates. If you crank `paths` all the way up to
40,000 and the page becomes sluggish, you are waiting on the browser
to re-histogram and re-plot, not on the simulator itself.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
## What to expect when you raise $\lambda_J$

With the compensated Merton drift, every book's **expected** P&L is
invariant in $(\lambda_J, \mu_J, \sigma_J)$ — the black mean rule on
each histogram stays where it is, and the "$\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$ (mc)"
column barely moves beyond its MC noise band. What does move:

- **SD, VaR, CVaR.** All widen, because jumps inject extra variance
  that GBM cannot represent.
- **Skew.** With $\mu_J < 0$ the principal (custom) and
  back-to-back histograms grow a longer left tail; the fee
  histogram a longer right tail (it is a mirror). With $\mu_J = 0$
  the widening is symmetric.
- **Sample paths.** The grey lines below pick up occasional visible
  discrete drops / gaps — the signature of Poisson-arrival jumps
  between grid points.

At $\lambda_J = 0$ the page is bit-identical to pure GBM, so use that
as the anchor when you want to read off the jump contribution.
:::

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
run = {
  const inputs = {
    S0: derived.S0, mu, sigma: derived.sigma, P,
    lambda: derived.lambda, T: derived.T,
    Q, fee, kPre, cBasis,
    lambdaJ, muJ, sigmaJ,
    nPaths, nSteps, seed,
  };
  const t0 = performance.now();
  const raw = simulate(inputs);
  const elapsedMs = performance.now() - t0;
  return {
    inputs,
    ...raw,
    fee: summarise(raw.feeSamples),
    principal: summarise(raw.principalSamples),
    b2b: summarise(raw.b2bSamples),
    elapsedMs,
  };
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
html`<div class="phase-c-derived">
  <div class="card"><div class="label">paths simulated</div><div class="value">${nPaths.toLocaleString()}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">wall time</div><div class="value">${run.elapsedMs.toFixed(1)} ms</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">coverage this run</div><div class="value">${(run.tauFrac * 100).toFixed(1)}%</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">leftover tokens at T</div><div class="value">${run.tokensLeftover.toLocaleString(undefined, {maximumFractionDigits: 0})}</div></div>
</div>`
```

### Summary statistics

Three books are simulated on each draw:

- **Fee** — $R_{\mathrm{fee}} = f \cdot P \cdot \lambda \cdot I_T$, revenue only.
- **Principal (back-to-back)** — $\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}} = Q \cdot N - P \cdot \lambda \cdot I_T$. No pre-purchase, for reference.
- **Principal (custom inventory)** — $\Pi_{\mathrm{cust}} = Q \cdot N - C_{\mathrm{basis}} - P \cdot \lambda \int_{\tau T}^{T} S_t\,dt + k_{\mathrm{left}} \cdot S_T$, the active slider model.

The **back-to-back** row is kept as a reference: it is the pure "no
pre-purchase" baseline against which the custom-inventory book's risk
reduction is measured. Equivalent to Phase B's `principal_3b`.

**Column guide.**

| column | meaning |
| --- | --- |
| $\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$ (mc) | expected P&L, estimated from the MC sample mean |
| SD | sample standard deviation of the P&L |
| $\pm \mathrm{CI}_{95}$ | 95% half-width of the **mean**'s confidence interval (= $1.96 \cdot \mathrm{SD} / \sqrt{n}$); shrinks with more paths |
| $\mathrm{VaR}_{95} / \mathrm{VaR}_{99}$ | loss threshold exceeded with 5% / 1% probability |
| $\mathrm{CVaR}_{95} / \mathrm{CVaR}_{99}$ | expected loss inside the worst 5% / 1% tail |
| $\mathbb{P}[\Pi < 0]$ | probability the book ends the horizon in the red |
| Sharpe | $\mathbb{E}[\Pi] / \mathrm{SD}[\Pi]$ — expected reward per unit of risk |

All P&L numbers are USD totals over the horizon $T$ (not annualised,
not per tonne). If you want a per-tonne figure, divide by $N = \lambda
T$ (printed in the derived-quantities card above).

**Reading tips.**

- Row **fee** is a revenue, always $\geq 0$. Its VaR/CVaR are therefore
  negative numbers — "the worst case is a smaller gain, not a loss
  against zero."
- Row **principal (custom)** is the row that responds to every slider
  change: shifting $Q$ moves its mean; shifting $\sigma$ widens its
  tails; shifting $k$ or $C_{\mathrm{basis}}$ changes either shape
  (via $\tau$ / $J_\tau$) or location (via the basis).
- Row **principal (back-to-back)** is fixed relative to $Q$ and the
  price process; it does **not** depend on $k$ or $C_{\mathrm{basis}}$.
  If the custom row's numbers converge to this row as you drag $k$
  toward zero, the simulator is implementing the Phase B identity
  $\Pi_{\mathrm{cust}}\big|_{k=0,\,C_{\mathrm{basis}}=0} = \Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}$
  correctly.
- When coverage hits 100% exactly, the custom book's SD and CVaR
  collapse to the sign of the one deterministic P&L — you should see
  SD approaching zero in the table.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
summaryRows = [
  { book: "fee", ...run.fee },
  { book: "principal (custom)", ...run.principal },
  { book: "principal (back-to-back)", ...run.b2b },
]
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(summaryRows, {
  columns: ["book","mean","sd","ci95","var95","var99","cvar95","cvar99","probLoss","sharpe"],
  header: {
    book: "book",
    mean: "E[Π] (mc)",
    sd: "SD",
    ci95: "±CI95",
    var95: "VaR95",
    var99: "VaR99",
    cvar95: "CVaR95",
    cvar99: "CVaR99",
    probLoss: "P[Π<0]",
    sharpe: "Sharpe",
  },
  format: {
    mean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    sd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    ci95: x => "±$" + x.toFixed(2),
    var95: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    var99: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    cvar95: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    cvar99: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    probLoss: x => x.toFixed(3),
    sharpe: x => x === null ? "—" : (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(3),
  }
})
```

### P&L distributions

The table above summarises each book with a handful of statistics; the
plot below shows the **full distribution** behind those statistics —
one panel per book.

How to read a panel:

- The **horizontal axis** is the book's terminal P&L in USD. Negative
  values are losses.
- The **bars** are a histogram of simulated P&Ls, binned into a
  proportion (so the three panels are directly comparable in shape
  even if their dollar scales differ).
- The **solid black vertical rule** is the MC-estimated mean
  $\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$ — the same number that appears in the table's
  "$\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$ (mc)" column. It should sit at the visual centre
  of the histogram if MC is converged.
- The **dashed grey rule** at $\Pi = 0$ is the break-even line. The
  mass of the histogram to the left of it is the loss probability
  $\mathbb{P}[\Pi < 0]$ reported in the table.

What to notice on each panel:

- **Fee.** Non-negative by construction; the histogram sits entirely
  to the right of $\Pi = 0$. Its shape inherits the right-skew of
  $I_T$ (the Phase B plot shows $I_T$'s histogram directly).
- **Principal (custom).** This is the one that changes as you drag
  sliders. At low coverage it is wide and left-skewed (the
  back-to-back regime); at 100% coverage it collapses to a spike (the
  matched regime); in between it interpolates. Over-hedging rebuilds
  the width but with a different shape.
- **Principal (back-to-back).** The mirror-image of the fee panel: same
  random kernel $I_T$, entered with a minus sign, so right-skew becomes
  left-skew. Useful as a visual anchor — the custom panel collapses to
  this one when $k = C_{\mathrm{basis}} = 0$.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
pnlRecords = {
  const rows = [];
  const push = (label, arr) => {
    for (let i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) rows.push({ book: label, pnl: arr[i] });
  };
  // Subsample for plotting responsiveness.
  const stride = Math.max(1, Math.floor(run.feeSamples.length / 2500));
  for (let i = 0; i < run.feeSamples.length; i += stride) {
    rows.push({ book: "fee", pnl: run.feeSamples[i] });
    rows.push({ book: "principal (custom)", pnl: run.principalSamples[i] });
    rows.push({ book: "principal (back-to-back)", pnl: run.b2bSamples[i] });
  }
  return rows;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 900,
  height: 440,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginRight: 30,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "terminal P&L (USD)", tickFormat: "$~s" },
  y: { label: "empirical density" },
  fy: { label: "book" },
  color: { legend: true, domain: ["fee","principal (custom)","principal (back-to-back)"] },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.ruleX([0], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "2 4" }),
    Plot.rectY(pnlRecords, Plot.binX({ y: "proportion" }, { x: "pnl", fill: "book", fy: "book", thresholds: 50 })),
    Plot.ruleX([
      { book: "fee", x: run.fee.mean },
      { book: "principal (custom)", x: run.principal.mean },
      { book: "principal (back-to-back)", x: run.b2b.mean },
    ], { x: "x", fy: "book", stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 2 }),
  ]
})
```

_Solid black: MC-estimated $\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$. Dashed grey: $\Pi = 0$. The
custom-principal panel collapses toward a spike as coverage approaches
100% — that's the §3a matched book limit._

### Sample price paths and inventory decay

Histograms show the **distribution** of outcomes; this plot shows a
handful of **individual** price trajectories so the reader gets an
intuition for the underlying stochastic process that produced them.

What to look for:

- **Grey lines** are ~20 randomly sampled GBM trajectories of the
  token spot. The density of grey at a given $(t, S_t)$ is a rough
  visual proxy for where the probability mass lives at that time.
- The **orange line** is the first trajectory, highlighted so a single
  path can be followed end-to-end.
- The **dashed green rule** marks the time at which the pre-purchased
  inventory runs out ($t = \tau T$). To the **left** of this line the
  operator is drawing tokens from inventory; to the **right**, buying
  at spot. The custom book's randomness is driven entirely by what
  happens to the grey lines to the **right** of this rule (and, if
  over-hedged, by where those lines land at $t = T$).
- If coverage is 100% the green rule sits at (or past) $t = T$ — the
  inventory never runs out and the back-to-back purchasing window is
  empty. If coverage is 0% the rule is at $t = 0$ and the whole
  horizon is back-to-back.
- All the trajectories start at the same $S_0$ (left edge) and fan out
  as the horizon advances, with the fan width growing like
  $\sigma\sqrt{t}$ — this is the visual signature of GBM and a useful
  sanity check on the simulator.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
pathRecords = {
  const rows = [];
  const paths = run.sampledPaths;
  const nS = run.inputs.nSteps;
  for (let idx = 0; idx < paths.length; idx++) {
    const path = paths[idx];
    for (let k = 0; k <= nS; k++) {
      rows.push({
        tDays: (k / nS) * Tdays,
        S: path[k],
        id: idx,
      });
    }
  }
  return rows;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 900,
  height: 340,
  marginLeft: 70,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "t (days)", domain: [0, Tdays], ticks: xTicksForHorizon(Tdays) },
  y: { label: "kVCM spot S_t (USD)", zero: true, tickFormat: "$~s" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(pathRecords, { x: "tDays", y: "S", z: "id", stroke: "#888", strokeOpacity: 0.35 }),
    Plot.line(pathRecords.filter(r => r.id === 0), { x: "tDays", y: "S", stroke: "#c94", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.ruleX([run.tauFrac * Tdays], { stroke: "#2a7", strokeDasharray: "4 3", strokeWidth: 2 }),
  ]
})
```

_Grey: sampled GBM trajectories of the kVCM spot. Orange: first path
highlighted. Green dashed rule: time at which the pre-purchased inventory
is exhausted — to the right of this line, retirements are sourced at
spot._

:::

::::

## Take-aways from this page

- **Coverage $\tau$ drives the custom book's shape; basis drives its
  location.** Dragging $k$ changes the width and skew of the
  Principal (custom) histogram; dragging $C_{\mathrm{basis}}$ just
  translates it along the P&L axis.
- **At 100% coverage the book collapses to a spike.** Exact Phase A
  §3a matched-book limit — SD on the custom row approaches zero and
  the histogram narrows to a single bin.
- **Crossing $k = N \cdot P$ changes the *kind* of risk.** Under-hedge
  loss comes from high prices via the averaged integral $J_\tau$
  (unbounded but softened loss tail). Over-hedge loss comes from low
  prices via $S_T$, which is bounded below by $0$ — so the loss tail
  is *bounded* and the heavy log-normal tail lands on the gain side.

For cross-phase business take-aways, scope caveats, implementation
notes, and a drawable price-curve stress-test tool, see the
[Conclusions](conclusions.qmd) page.
